one_line_takeaway: By modeling MCP attacks as component-level 'influence paths' rather than effect-based categories, the authors show that direct code injection is universally successful (100% ASR) and multi-component/cross-stage composition reliably evades LLM safeguards, then build Connor -- a trajectory-aware, intent-baseline behavioral deviation detector -- which beats three open-source MCP security scanners by 8.9-59.6 F1 points (94.6% F1) on a 134-malicious/130-benign curated dataset.
key_finding: Attack-side findings (Table 5, Section 4.2): (1) Direct code injection into tool source code, multi-tool source code, or server configuration (paths P17, P18, P19) achieves 100% ASR across every tested host-LLM configuration, attributed to the absence of source-code validation in current MCP hosts. (2) Tool description (TD) and argument schema (AS) are empirically equivalent injection points (P1 vs P2, P4 vs P5 show comparable ASR), validating the paper's de-duplication of semantically equivalent paths. (3) Multi-component attacks that split malicious logic across components outperform single-component attacks -- e.g., P3 and P7 (combining prompt injection with source-code-triggered logic) achieve 66.7%-100% ASR versus lower rates for pure prompt-injection path P1. (4) Pre-execution artifacts (TD/AS) are more vulnerable to injection than post-execution artifacts (TR): in Cursor, response-based injection P8 scores 3.3%-63.3% ASR versus description-based P1 at 40.0%-66.7%. (5) Claude Desktop shows markedly stronger host-level defenses than Cursor -- 0% ASR for builtin-operation prompt injections (P1, P2, P8, P16) versus Cursor's 3.3%-66.7%; and LLM security behavior is host-dependent, not purely LLM-intrinsic (e.g., Sonnet 4.5 is more resistant than Sonnet 4.0 in Cursor but less resistant in Claude Desktop). (6) GPT-5 shows the strongest resilience to response-based injections among the five LLMs tested; DeepSeek 3.1 trusts injected content only when a tool response contains solely the injection payload (no legitimate content mixed in). (7) Extending 2-stage attack chains to 3-stage chains consistently lowers ASR (overall average 68.7% -> 58.0% across P11/P13/P15 and their extended counterparts), empirically supporting the paper's bounded-chain design rationale. Defense-side finding (Table 7, Section 6.2): Connor achieves Precision 98.4% / Recall 91.1% / F1 94.6%, outperforming MCP-Scan (59.3% F1), MCPScan (72.1% F1), and AI-Infra-Guard (86.2% F1) -- an improvement of 8.9 to 59.6 percentage points in F1 over the three baselines. Connor produced only 2 false positives (a music-download tool flagged for unconstrained network requests; an image-processing tool whose empty response caused the agent to fall back to builtin command execution, misread as payload delivery). In real-world deployment on the remaining 1,672 marketplace servers, Connor flagged and (per the text visible in the extracted pages) the authors report identifying two genuinely malicious servers upon manual verification.
core_contribution: The paper (Huang, Zhao, Chen, Wu, Zhou, Cao, Hu, Peng; Fudan University) makes two distinct contributions. First, it introduces a component-centric perspective on malicious Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, arguing that prior taxonomies classify attacks by observable effect (e.g., 'prompt injection', 'tool poisoning') rather than by which MCP server component (tool description, argument schema, tool source code, tool response, resource code/response, or server configuration) is manipulated and how manipulated components chain together. To operationalize this, the authors formally define 'influence paths' via a canonical signature (Med, Stage, Sink, Carrier), systematically enumerate 26 feasible paths, de-duplicate to 16 semantically independent paths, and instantiate 19 influence paths x 6 attack goals (data leakage, reverse shell, download-and-execute, ransomware, sabotaging, backdoor) = 114 PoC malicious MCP servers -- the first component-centric PoC dataset of its kind. Second, motivated by the finding that existing defenses are component-isolated and signature-restricted, the authors build Connor, a two-stage behavioral-deviation detector: a pre-execution stage (Config Analyzer for malicious shell commands in server config; Intent Inspector that separates a tool's declared function intent from any injected/adversarial intent in its description or argument schema) followed by an in-execution stage that runs the server in a simulated host (built on LangChain, with 13 emulated Cursor-style builtin tools), uses an Intent-Aligned Query Generator to synthesize diverse triggering queries, traces execution with an Execution Tracer, extracts code semantics via Joern-based CPG/PDG slicing (parameter-, return-value-, and sensitive-API-based slicing) through a Code Semantic Generator, and finally applies a Behavior Deviation Judger that performs trajectory-level (not just single-step) deviation detection against the function-intent baseline, issuing ALLOW/WARN/BLOCK verdicts with accumulating execution history across steps.
threat_model: Adversaries who craft and publish malicious MCP servers on legitimate third-party marketplaces (analogous to malicious NPM/PyPI packages), appearing benign/useful to induce installation. The paper explicitly contrasts this with traditional supply-chain attacks along four axes stated in the text: (1) a 'dual-channel threat model' where code-level supply-chain attack and prompt-injection attack coexist in one artifact; (2) 'component exposure to LLM reasoning' -- MCP metadata (tool descriptions, argument schemas) is directly consumed by the LLM as reasoning context, unlike inert package README files; (3) 'cross-component influence chains via LLM orchestration' where adversarial content in one component can propagate through LLM reasoning to trigger a different component; and (4) 'attacks via LLM decision manipulation' where system compromise can occur without the malicious server executing any code itself, by steering the LLM to abuse the host's own builtin tools (the malicious server as 'attack catalyst rather than attack executor'). Assumptions stated by the authors: benign user inputs, uncompromised host applications and LLMs, attackers have full control over their own server's implementation (tool metadata, tool code, resource contents, server configuration) while remaining protocol-compliant, attackers cannot modify the MCP spec, intercept network traffic, or directly compromise hosts/LLMs. Server-provided prompt templates are explicitly excluded as an attack vector because their retrieval is user/host-initiated, not LLM-automatic. The scope is intra-server, at most two tools, at most three injected components, at most one resource access, at most one builtin-tool invocation, and at most two LLM interaction rounds per PoC (justified both practically -- combinatorial blowup of expert-crafted PoCs -- and empirically, since Section 4.2.3 shows extending 2-stage to 3-stage chains reduces average ASR from 68.7% to 58.0%).
limitations: Limitations explicitly acknowledged or evident from the paper's own text: (1) The PoC dataset is explicitly stated to be mechanism-oriented, not representative of real-world malicious-server distribution -- the authors note only a handful of real-world malicious MCP servers have been publicly disclosed, making distributional claims infeasible. (2) The threat model excludes inter-server attacks (multi-server attack chains), explicitly left to future work. (3) Server-provided prompt templates are excluded as an attack surface by threat-model construction, not because they were shown to be unexploitable. (4) Attack chains are bounded at 2 tools / 3 injected components / 1 resource access / 1 builtin-tool call / 2 LLM rounds, justified partly by combinatorial infeasibility of manual expert PoC construction rather than purely by attacker-realism arguments (though the paper does provide an empirical ASR-decay argument in Section 4.2.3 for chains beyond 2 stages). (5) Connor's design relies heavily on LLM judges (GPT-5) at multiple pipeline stages (config analysis, intent extraction, code-semantic extraction, deviation judging), which the paper does not evaluate for adversarial robustness against an attacker who specifically targets the LLM-judge components themselves (e.g., via prompts designed to fool the Intent Inspector or Behavior Deviation Judger), despite the threat model conceding attackers have full control over server content that these very LLM judges consume. (6) Connor's own false-positive cases (music-download and image-processing tools) suggest the deviation baseline can misfire on legitimate but loosely-scoped tools. (7) Connor targets stdio-based MCP servers specifically (stated as the majority deployment mode), so its applicability to remote/HTTP-based MCP servers is not directly evaluated in the paper. (8) The evaluation compares against 3 baselines reconfigured to use GPT-5, which somewhat controls for base-model effects but the comparison protocol (treating 'any high risk alert' or 'any security alert' as malicious for baselines) could be a source of asymmetric sensitivity not fully explored.

datasets_used: 114 self-constructed component-centric PoC malicious MCP servers (19 influence paths x 6 attack goals)

20 additional malicious PoC servers drawn from prior work (Hou et al., Song et al., and [72]), filtered down from 8+ candidates in Hou et al. per stated exclusion criteria, combined with the 114 for a total of 134 malicious instances used in detector evaluation

130 manually-verified benign MCP servers, randomly sampled from 1,802 self-contained executable candidates filtered from 13,526 valid servers crawled (via 'MCP Crawler') from six marketplaces: MCP.so, PulseMCP, Smithery, Glama, Cursor.directory, and MCP Servers (crawl date before April 2026)
1,672 remaining real-world marketplace MCP servers used for the real-world deployment/usefulness evaluation of Connor
quantitative_results: Attack effectiveness (Table 5): P17/P18/P19 (direct code/config injection) = 100% ASR across all 7 tested host-LLM configurations
Attack effectiveness: Claude Desktop + Sonnet 4.0/4.5 show 0% ASR for prompt injections targeting builtin operations (P1, P2, P8), versus 3.3%-66.7% ASR for the same paths on Cursor

Chain-length ablation (Table 6): average ASR across P11/P13/P15 and 3-stage extensions drops from 84.0% to 72.0% (P11 family, data leakage), 40.0% to 20.0% (P13 family, data leakage), 88.0% to 80.0% (P15 family, data leakage); sabotaging goal shows 92.0%->88.0%, 28.0%->12.0%, 80.0%->76.0% respectively; overall average ASR decreases from 68.7% to 58.0%

Detector effectiveness (Table 7): Connor Precision 98.4%, Recall 91.1%, F1 94.6%; MCP-Scan Precision 60.5%, Recall 58.2%, F1 59.3%; AI-Infra-Guard Precision 81.8%, Recall 91.0%, F1 86.2%; MCPScan Precision 75.0%, Recall 69.4%, F1 72.1%
Connor F1 improvement over baselines: 8.9 to 59.6 percentage points
Connor produced exactly 2 false positives on the curated dataset
Connor implementation size: 9,000 lines of Python code

Real-world deployment: Connor evaluated on 1,672 remaining marketplace servers and identified malicious servers among them (the extracted text -- abstract and intro -- states 'Connor identifies two malicious servers'; the RQ6 results table itself was beyond the 22-page extraction window used for this review)
Sensitive-API list used in Code Semantic Generator's slicing: 286 Python and 247 JavaScript sensitive APIs
425 risk-token patterns collected for the Config Analyzer from grey literature and prior studies

26 total feasible influence paths enumerated by the canonical signature; collapsed to 16 semantically independent paths after de-duplication; 19 paths ultimately used for PoC construction (16 + 2 AS-based equivalence-verification counterparts P2/P5 + 1 CONFIG path P19)
baselines_compared: AI-Infra-Guard [66] -- F1 86.2% (reconfigured to use GPT-5 as base LLM)
MCPScan [2] -- F1 72.1% (reconfigured to use GPT-5 as base LLM)
